items.q: Can you stop a foreclosure in Florida?
items.a: Yes. In Florida, options exist to halt foreclosure — including evaluating a cash-offer path that can close in 7–21 days. We tailor the approach to your situation and help you understand the timeline and your rights under Florida law.

items.q: What is the fastest way to stop a foreclosure?
items.a: The fastest path is typically a cash sale that can close in days, avoiding repairs and agent commissions. A cash buyer can make an offer quickly and close before the foreclosure sale date, giving you certainty and immediate relief.

items.q: What is the 120-day rule for foreclosure?
items.a: Foreclosure timelines vary. The 120-day period is a common reference window in which lender actions may occur after missed payments, but specific timelines depend on your lender, loan type, and state law. Consult a local attorney or HUD counselor for guidance specific to your case.

items.q: How many mortgage payments can you miss before foreclosure in Florida?
items.a: There is no single fixed number. Missed payments trigger lender actions, but options like counseling, modifications, or cash offers can alter timing. Generally, foreclosure proceedings may begin after several months of missed payments — acting early gives you more options.

items.q: Is my cash offer calculation free?
items.a: Yes — completely free and no-obligation. We evaluate your property in person, review local comps, and give you a written cash offer. You're under no pressure to accept and there are no costs to you for requesting the evaluation.

items.q: Are there any fees or commissions?
items.a: None. We don't charge commissions, agent fees, or closing costs. The number in your offer is the number you receive at closing — no deductions, no surprises.

items.q: What kinds of homes do you buy?
items.a: Any condition — foreclosures, fixer-uppers, inherited homes, vacant properties, homes with tenants, fire-damaged homes, and more. We buy as-is in St Petersburg, Clearwater, Largo, and across the Greater Tampa Bay area.

items.q: What government programs can help stop foreclosure?
items.a: Federal and state foreclosure-prevention resources include HUD-certified counselors, mortgage forbearance and modification programs, and the HOPE NOW Alliance. We can point you to official programs and counselors to help you explore all options — not just a cash sale.

items.question: Is probate required to sell an inherited property?
items.answer: Probate is typically required when a property passes through an estate without joint ownership or a trust. If the deceased owned the property solely in their name, probate is necessary to transfer the title to the heirs — this applies to most inherited properties in St Petersburg and throughout Florida.

items.question: How long does Florida probate take?

Formal administration typically takes 6–12 months or longer. In Pinellas County, court scheduling and backlog affect timelines — but our familiarity with local procedures helps us identify which steps can be accelerated and what documentation to prepare in advance.

tax.lede: Florida has no state inheritance tax — but federal capital gains rules still apply. Here's a general overview to help you plan ahead.
paragraphs: Florida does not have a state inheritance or estate tax, which simplifies the process for most heirs. However, federal estate tax may apply to very large estates, and capital gains tax considerations depend on the property's stepped-up basis at the time of inheritance.

When you inherit property, the IRS typically resets the property's basis to its fair market value at the date of the decedent's death — this is the "stepped-up basis." This often means little to no capital gains tax if you sell the property shortly after inheriting it, since the basis and sale price are close together.

If the property appreciates significantly after inheritance, you may owe capital gains tax on the difference between the stepped-up basis and the eventual sale price. A cash sale that closes quickly can minimize this exposure.
tax.disclaimer: Tax questions are best answered by a qualified CPA or tax attorney familiar with Florida real estate. The information above is general in nature and does not constitute tax advice.

prose.lede: Equitable distribution is Florida's method of dividing marital property — not necessarily in equal shares, but fairly. This framework governs how home sale proceeds are split during a Florida divorce.
paragraphs: According to Florida Statute 61.075, the court considers factors like the length of the marriage, each spouse's economic circumstances, and contributions to the marital property. This process directly affects sale timing.

If both spouses agree on a sale, you can move forward quickly. If you can't agree, the court may step in — extending the timeline by weeks or months. Working with a cash buyer from the start can simplify the division process and reduce the risk of delays.

We work with divorcing couples across St. Petersburg and Tampa Bay to provide a straightforward cash-sale path that aligns with equitable distribution goals — no commissions, no repairs, no drawn-out listing periods.

items.q: What happens to the lease when the property is sold in Florida?
items.a: The current lease generally survives the sale, and the buyer takes over as landlord under the existing lease terms. Florida law protects tenant occupancy rights — the new owner inherits all landlord responsibilities and the tenant continues living there through the remainder of their lease period.

items.q: What is cash-for-keys and does it speed up the closing?
items.a: Cash-for-keys is a voluntary agreement where a tenant vacates early in exchange for a relocation payment. We may offer tenants this incentive when vacant possession accelerates the close or benefits all parties. This voluntary arrangement benefits everyone — you close faster, tenants receive compensation, and the buyer gets clear possession.

items.q: What happens to the security deposits at closing?
items.a: Security deposits must either transfer to the buyer (with written notice to tenants) or return to tenants with proper accounting. Florida statutes require written notice identifying the new owner and confirming the deposit amount. We coordinate this transfer seamlessly during closing to protect you from post-sale liability.

items.q: What documents should I prepare for a tenant-occupied Florida sale?
items.a: Prepare: current lease agreements, rent payment history, security deposit amounts, any outstanding maintenance requests or disputes, and any existing tenant notices. We guide you through the full documentation process — you don't need to figure it out alone.

items.q: Do buyers care about tenancy when purchasing a rental with tenants in place?
items.a: Investor buyers often view tenant occupancy positively — it means immediate rental income, no lease-up period, and proven tenant demand. We evaluate lease quality and payment history, but we buy properties with tenants regardless of these factors because we're equipped to handle any situation.

items.q: Why choose We Buy St Pete Houses for selling tenant-occupied Florida property?
items.a: Local expertise, cash offers, no commissions, and a fast, transparent closing process. We specialize in tenant-occupied properties across St. Petersburg and Tampa Bay. prose.lede: Lease terms, deposit handling, and transfer mechanics determine how quickly a sale can close when tenants are present. Here's exactly how it works in Florida.
paragraphs: When you sell a rental property with tenants in Florida, the existing lease doesn't simply disappear — it transfers to the new owner along with all its terms and conditions. The buyer steps into your shoes as landlord, inheriting the current rent amount, lease duration, and security deposit obligations.

Security deposits must be handled carefully at closing. Florida landlord-tenant statutes require that deposits either transfer to the new owner or return to the tenant, with proper documentation and notice. We coordinate this transfer seamlessly, protecting you from post-close liability.

Notice requirements vary based on lease type. Month-to-month tenancies typically require proper notice before showing or selling, while fixed-term leases continue until expiration unless both parties agree otherwise. We navigate these requirements so you can sell without legal complications or tenant disputes.

Cash buyers like us often prefer tenant-occupied properties because they generate immediate rental income. We can close in as little as 7 days, with or without tenants in place, because we buy as-is and handle all tenant coordination directly.

items.q: Can I sell my house as-is in Florida?
items.a: Yes. Selling a house as-is in Florida is a common and legal option. We buy homes as-is for cash, handling properties in any condition without requiring you to make repairs. We close quickly and transparently, often within 7 days.

items.q: How fast can I sell my house as-is in Florida?
items.a: We can close in as little as 7 days, depending on your schedule and title clearance. Most as-is cash sales complete within 7 to 14 days — far faster than traditional listings which average 30 to 60+ days.

items.q: What is the Florida as-is contract?
items.a: The Florida as-is contract is a standard agreement (using FAR/BAR forms) stating the property is sold in its current condition with no repair obligations for the seller. The buyer accepts the home as it stands. We explain every clause before you sign so there are no surprises.

items.q: Are there hidden fees or costs in a cash sale?
items.a: No hidden fees. There are no agent commissions in a direct cash sale. Standard closing costs such as title and recording fees may apply, but we explain all costs upfront so you know exactly what to expect before you sign anything.

items.q: What steps are involved to close a Florida cash sale?
items.a: The process: 1) Contact us with property details, 2) Receive a cash offer within 24 hours, 3) Review and accept if it works for you, 4) Sign the Florida as-is contract, 5) We coordinate title work with a local title company, 6) Move out on your schedule, 7) Close and receive payment. items.q: Do I need a lawyer to sell my home as-is in Florida?

items.q: What happens if the home has HOA issues or liens?
items.a: We address HOA dues and liens during our evaluation. Cash buyers work directly with title companies to resolve these issues before closing — simplifying your responsibilities and ensuring a smooth transaction without delays.

items.q: Is it better to sell as-is or fix the house up first?
items.a: It depends on your priorities. If you need to move quickly, avoid repair costs, and value certainty, selling as-is for cash makes sense. If maximizing sale price is your only goal and you have time and money to invest in repairs, a traditional listing may yield more. For most time-pressured sellers, the speed and simplicity of a cash sale outweigh the price difference.

items.q: How can I compare a cash offer vs. listing with repairs in Florida?

Net proceeds are often closer than they appear.
